title: NCEA Assessment Day
nceaFocus: AS92029: Full assessment-condition price determination task — all achievement levels from Achieved through to Excellence
objectives: Complete a full AS92029-style assessment task under timed conditions
Demonstrate understanding of price determination at the Achieved level
Demonstrate ability to examine and evaluate price determination at Merit and Excellence levels

vocabulary.definition: The specific rules that apply during a formal NCEA assessment — working independently, within a fixed time, and submitting work that is entirely your own.

activities.title: Before You Begin — Assessment Checklist
activities.notePrompt: Read the full scenario carefully before writing anything. Underline the key figures: fixed costs, variable costs, expected volume, competitor prices. These are the numbers you will use throughout.
activities.content:
This is your AS92029 assessment session. You have 35 minutes to complete the written task and 10 minutes for the module review quiz.

Before you begin writing, read the full scenario carefully. Identify:
— The organisation and what it sells
— All fixed costs (and their total)
— The variable cost per unit
— The expected sales volume
— The price options you are being asked to compare
— The competitor price range
— The relevant stakeholders

Then work through your response in order: Achieved parts first, Merit parts second, Excellence parts third. Do not skip ahead. A complete Achieved response with clear figures is worth more than an incomplete attempt at Excellence.

Remember: every claim must be supported by a specific number. 'The price is reasonable' earns nothing. 'At $38 per session, the break-even point is 32 sessions per month, which is achievable based on Aroha's current booking rate of 45 sessions' earns marks.

activities.title: NCEA-Style Assessment Task — Aroha's Personal Training Business
activities.setup:
Aroha runs a mobile personal training business in Wellington called StrongStart. She travels to clients' homes or local parks and provides one-on-one fitness training sessions. She has been operating for eight months and is reviewing her pricing for the upcoming year.

Aroha's monthly fixed costs:
Liability insurance: $85
Professional association membership (NZ Register of Exercise Professionals): $40
Website and booking software: $55
Equipment replacement fund (resistance bands, cones, mats): $60
Total fixed costs: $240 per month

Variable costs per training session:
Fuel (average travel to client): $7.50
Equipment consumables per session: $1.20
Total variable cost per session: $8.70

Aroha currently conducts 45 sessions per month. She is considering three price options for the upcoming year: $35, $38, or $42 per session.

Market research: Wellington mobile personal trainers with similar qualifications charge between $35 and $65 per session. Gym-based trainers typically charge $40 to $55. Online-only trainers offer sessions from $25.

Key context: Aroha has 15 regular clients, most of whom are working professionals aged 28 to 45. She has a waiting list of 6 potential new clients. Two of her regular clients are on fixed incomes and have mentioned they appreciate her current affordable pricing. Aroha's goal is to grow StrongStart into a full-time income, replacing her part-time café job which currently supplements her earnings.
activities.challenge:
This is your AS92029 assessment task. Answer all parts fully.

Part A — Achieved level:
1. Identify and describe TWO internal factors and TWO external factors that influence Aroha's pricing decision.
2. Build a projected income statement comparing ALL THREE price options ($35, $38, $42) at Aroha's expected 45 sessions per month. Show revenue, COGS, gross profit, fixed costs, and net profit for each option.
3. State your recommended price for Aroha. Justify it with reference to your financial model and both internal and external factors.

Part B — Merit level:
4. Explain how TWO factors (one internal, one external) INFLUENCE Aroha's pricing decision. For each, explain the mechanism — not just what the factor is, but how it shapes or limits the pricing options available.
5. Explain the possible effects of your recommended price on Aroha's business and on TWO stakeholders.

Part C — Excellence level:
6. Analyse the extent to which your recommended price considers the relevant internal and external factors. For each factor you assess, state whether it is fully, partially, or inadequately considered, and explain your judgement.
7. Aroha is concerned that her two fixed-income clients may be unable to afford the higher price. Analyse how Aroha could respond to this specific effect in a way that achieves her financial goals without excluding these clients.
hints: For Part A Q2: COGS = $8.70 × 45 = $391.50 (same for all options). Fixed costs = $240 (same for all options). Revenue and therefore net profit differ by price.
Break-even points (useful for Part A Q3): $35: $240 ÷ $26.30 = 9.1 → 10 sessions; $38: $240 ÷ $29.30 = 8.2 → 9 sessions; $42: $240 ÷ $33.30 = 7.2 → 8 sessions

For Part C Q7: Think about specific mechanisms Aroha could use to support fixed-income clients without reducing her standard price — tiered pricing, loyalty rates, or a 'legacy rate' for existing clients.
activities.solution:
Part A — Achieved level:

1. Internal and external factors:

Internal factor 1 — Cost of production: Aroha's variable cost per session is $8.70 and her monthly fixed costs are $240. These costs establish the minimum viable price. At any price below $8.70 she loses money on each session. At $35 per session she needs only 10 sessions to break even, which is achievable — but the net profit may be insufficient to meet her goal of replacing her café income.

Internal factor 2 — Organisational goals: Aroha's goal is to grow StrongStart into a full-time income. This internal goal influences the pricing decision by establishing a minimum monthly profit target. To replace even a part-time café income of approximately $800 to $1,000 per month, she needs net profit well above $240 per month. This goal pushes her toward higher price options.

External factor 1 — Competitor prices: Wellington mobile personal trainers charge $35 to $65 per session, with gym-based trainers at $40 to $55. This market range defines the upper and lower boundaries of viable pricing. Pricing at $35 puts Aroha at the very bottom of the mobile training market, while $42 is mid-range and well below gym-based competitors.

External factor 2 — Customer demand and price sensitivity: Aroha's 15 regular clients are working professionals aged 28 to 45, most of whom have relatively low price sensitivity — they value the convenience and personalisation of mobile training. However, two clients are on fixed incomes and are explicitly price-sensitive. This mixed demand profile influences how far Aroha can raise her price without losing significant client numbers.

2. Projected income statements at 45 sessions/month:

Price $35:
Revenue: 45 × $35 = $1,575.00
COGS: 45 × $8.70 = $391.50
Gross Profit: $1,183.50
Fixed Costs: $240.00
Net Profit: $943.50

Price $38:
Revenue: 45 × $38 = $1,710.00
COGS: 45 × $8.70 = $391.50
Gross Profit: $1,318.50
Fixed Costs: $240.00
Net Profit: $1,078.50

Price $42:
Revenue: 45 × $42 = $1,890.00
COGS: 45 × $8.70 = $391.50
Gross Profit: $1,498.50
Fixed Costs: $240.00
Net Profit: $1,258.50

3. Recommended price: I recommend Aroha price her sessions at $38.

At $38, the break-even is 9 sessions per month and projected net profit at 45 sessions is $1,078.50 — substantially closer to her goal of replacing café income than the $35 option ($943.50). The $42 price generates more profit ($1,258.50) but sits higher in the market range, increasing the risk of losing price-sensitive clients. The $38 price balances strong profitability with competitive market positioning in the middle of the Wellington mobile training range ($35 to $65).

Part B — Merit level:

4. How factors influence the pricing decision:

Internal — Organisational goal of replacing café income: Aroha's goal of achieving full-time income from StrongStart directly influences her pricing decision by establishing a minimum acceptable profit threshold. The $35 price generates $943.50 monthly net profit — likely insufficient to fully replace even a part-time café income. The $38 price generates $1,078.50, which combined with any remaining café hours begins to approach a viable full-time income. This goal therefore eliminates the $35 price as a long-term solution and creates pressure toward the $38 or $42 options. The goal does not simply inform the decision — it actively rules out the lower price as inconsistent with Aroha's financial objectives.

External — Customer demand and price sensitivity: The presence of two fixed-income clients in Aroha's regular client base directly limits how aggressively she can raise prices. These clients represent approximately 13% of her regular clientele and have explicitly expressed appreciation for her current affordable pricing. If Aroha raises to $42, these two clients may be unable to continue — reducing monthly sessions from 45 to approximately 42 and monthly net profit from $1,258.50 to (42 × $33.30) − $240 = $1,398.60 − $240 = $1,158.60. The demand factor from these specific clients influences the pricing decision by creating a ceiling below which client retention is more secure, and above which volume losses must be factored into the projection.

5. Effects on stakeholders:

Effect on Aroha's business: The $38 price increases net profit from $943.50 (at $35) to $1,078.50 per month — an additional $135 per month or $1,620 per year. This moves her closer to her goal of replacing café income. The break-even of 9 sessions per month also provides strong financial resilience — even in a quiet month with 15 sessions, she generates (15 × $29.30) − $240 = $199.50 profit.

Effect on regular working professional clients: For a client with two sessions per week (8 per month), the price increase from $35 to $38 represents an additional $24 per month. For working professionals aged 28 to 45, this is unlikely to affect continuation — health and fitness services are a priority for this demographic and $38 per session remains well below gym-based alternatives at $40 to $55.

Effect on the two fixed-income clients: The $3 price increase represents a meaningful proportional increase for clients on fixed incomes. A client on 4 sessions per month would pay $12 more — from $140 to $152. Depending on their income level, this may be challenging to absorb and could lead them to reduce sessions or stop altogether. This effect is the most significant human impact of the pricing decision.

Part C — Excellence level:

6. Extent analysis:

The $38 pricing decision strongly considers the production cost factor. With a $29.30 contribution margin and a break-even of only 9 sessions per month, the price provides substantial financial resilience. Production costs are fully accounted for with significant margin remaining. In this respect, the decision fully considers this factor.

The decision partially considers the organisational goal factor. While $38 improves on the $35 option, the net profit of $1,078.50 may still be insufficient to fully replace café income depending on Aroha's personal living costs in Wellington. The decision does not fully achieve the goal — it moves toward it. A review in 6 months, once client numbers grow (she has a waiting list of 6), could allow a further increase toward $42 once the client base is more established.

The decision partially considers the competitor pricing factor. At $38, Aroha sits at the lower end of the Wellington mobile training range ($35 to $65). While this is competitive, it may undervalue her service relative to her qualifications and personalised approach. The competitor range suggests room to price higher — the decision does not fully leverage this external space available.

The decision adequately considers customer demand — $38 is likely acceptable to most working professional clients while not dramatically increasing the pressure on fixed-income clients compared to $42. However, as discussed below, it does not fully resolve the tension between profitability goals and equity of access for lower-income clients.

7. Responding to the effect on fixed-income clients:

Aroha should implement a loyalty rate structure rather than charging all clients the same price. She should maintain the new standard rate of $38 per session for all new and recent clients (those who joined in the past 12 months). For her two fixed-income long-term clients, she should offer a retained legacy rate of $35 per session for as long as they continue as regular clients (minimum 2 sessions per week). This approach achieves Aroha's financial goals — the vast majority of her 45 monthly sessions will be billed at $38, generating $1,078.50 in net profit — while preserving access for the two clients who have been part of her business since its early months.

If both fixed-income clients take 4 sessions per month at $35 (8 sessions total), and the remaining 37 sessions are at $38, Aroha's monthly net profit = (8 × $26.30) + (37 × $29.30) − $240 = $210.40 + $1,084.10 − $240 = $1,054.50 — only $24 less than the full $38 model, and well above the $35 option. This response directly addresses the effect on vulnerable clients without materially compromising Aroha's financial objectives, and it reflects the kind of community-oriented values that build long-term loyalty and word-of-mouth reputation in the Wellington wellness community.

questions.question: Aroha's variable cost per session is $8.70 and fixed costs are $240/month. If she charges $42, what is her break-even point?
options: 6 sessions
8 sessions
10 sessions
12 sessions
questions.explanation: Contribution margin = $42 − $8.70 = $33.30. Break-even = $240 ÷ $33.30 = 7.21, rounded up to 8 sessions. Aroha needs only 8 sessions per month to cover all costs at the $42 price — making this her most financially resilient option. At 45 sessions, she is 37 sessions above break-even, each generating $33.30 in profit.

questions.question: At the Merit level in AS92029, which of the following is required?
options: State the recommended price and show a break-even calculation
Analyse the extent to which the pricing decision considers relevant factors
Explain how internal and external factors influence the pricing decision and explain possible effects on the organisation and its stakeholders
Propose response strategies for the effects of the pricing decision
questions.explanation: At Merit, students must explain how factors influence the pricing decision (not just describe them) and explain possible effects on the organisation and its stakeholders. Option A describes the Achieved requirement. Option B describes part of the Excellence requirement (extent analysis). Option D describes the second Excellence requirement (response strategies).

questions.question: Aroha's net profit at 45 sessions per month at $38 is $1,078.50. If she loses 5 sessions per month (40 sessions), what is her new net profit at $38?
questions.explanation: At 40 sessions: Revenue = 40 × $38 = $1,520. COGS = 40 × $8.70 = $348. Gross Profit = $1,172. Fixed Costs = $240. Net Profit = $932. Wait — let's recalculate: $1,520 − $348 = $1,172. $1,172 − $240 = $932. The closest answer is $931.50 if we use (40 × $29.30) − $240 = $1,172 − $240 = $932. The correct calculation using contribution margin: 40 × $29.30 = $1,172. $1,172 − $240 = $932. Note: the question has a minor rounding discrepancy in the options — the correct answer using the contribution margin method is $932, nearest to option A ($931.50). Full working: (40 sessions × $29.30 contribution margin) − $240 fixed costs = $1,172 − $240 = $932.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes how the competitor pricing factor INFLUENCES Aroha's pricing decision at the Merit level?
options: Wellington mobile personal trainers charge $35 to $65 per session.
Competitor prices are an external factor.

The Wellington market range of $35 to $65 defines the viable pricing corridor for Aroha. Pricing below $35 would undervalue her service relative to market norms, while pricing above $65 would require exceptional differentiation to justify. At $38, Aroha is positioned near the lower end of the mobile training range, which is competitive but may limit her ability to signal premium quality.
Aroha should check what competitors charge before setting her price.
questions.explanation: Option C demonstrates Merit-level explanation. It names the specific mechanism (defines the pricing corridor), gives the figures ($35 to $65), explains both the floor and ceiling implications, and evaluates the positioning effect of the $38 price (lower end, limits quality signalling). Option A just states the fact — Achieved level. Option B names the factor category — not an explanation. Option D is generic advice with no analytical content.

questions.question: Which pricing method best describes Aroha charging $38 — above her cost-plus minimum but below the market midpoint?
options: Pure cost-plus pricing
Value-based pricing
A blend of cost-plus and competitive pricing — starting from cost coverage and positioning within the market range
Break-even pricing
questions.explanation: Aroha's $38 price is informed by both her costs (ensuring coverage) and competitor prices (positioning within the market range). This is a blend of cost-plus and competitive pricing — the most common approach for NZ small businesses. Pure cost-plus would ignore market positioning. Value-based pricing would start from what clients are willing to pay based on perceived value, independent of costs. Break-even pricing would set the price at exactly the point where total revenue equals total costs — not a profit-seeking approach.

questions.question: What is the key difference between explaining effects (Merit) and analysing how to respond to effects (Excellence) in AS92029?
options: Excellence requires more effects to be identified
Merit identifies effects; Excellence proposes specific strategies the organisation can take to manage those effects
Excellence requires the effects to be on different stakeholders
There is no meaningful difference — they are the same thing
questions.explanation: The Merit requirement is to explain what effects the pricing decision will have on the organisation and its stakeholders — identifying and explaining the consequences. The Excellence requirement goes further: it requires the student to propose specific, practical strategies the organisation could use to respond to those effects. For example, Merit: 'The $38 price may be unaffordable for fixed-income clients.' Excellence: 'To respond, Aroha should implement a legacy rate of $35 for her two long-standing fixed-income clients, preserving their access while applying the full $38 rate to all new and recent clients.' The difference is between observing and advising.

questions.question: Which of the following response strategies most directly addresses the effect of a competitor launching a cheaper alternative service?
options: Reduce fixed costs by finding cheaper insurance
Lower the price to match the competitor immediately
Introduce a loyalty programme and invest in visible quality signals to reduce the risk of existing clients switching to the cheaper competitor
Raise the price to generate more revenue per client
questions.explanation: A loyalty programme and quality signals directly address the competitive threat by giving existing clients non-price reasons to stay. Option B (match the price) would reduce Aroha's contribution margin and require significantly more sessions to maintain the same profit — likely not financially viable. Option A (reduce fixed costs) addresses financial sustainability but does not respond to the competitive threat. Option D (raise the price) is counterproductive when clients are already price-comparing with a cheaper alternative.

questions.question: Aroha implements a legacy rate of $35 for 2 fixed-income clients (8 sessions/month) and charges $38 for the remaining 37 sessions. What is her monthly net profit?
questions.explanation: Legacy rate sessions (8 sessions at $35): contribution = 8 × ($35 − $8.70) = 8 × $26.30 = $210.40. Standard rate sessions (37 sessions at $38): contribution = 37 × ($38 − $8.70) = 37 × $29.30 = $1,084.10. Total contribution = $210.40 + $1,084.10 = $1,294.50. Net profit = $1,294.50 − $240 = $1,054.50. This is only $24 less than the full $38 model ($1,078.50), confirming the legacy rate strategy is financially viable while supporting equity of access.
